title: Constants
description: Values that aren't allowed to change: the const keyword, the older #define approach, naming convention, and why constants matter.
objectives: Understand what a constant is and how it differs from a variable
Recognize the #define preprocessor approach and how it differs from const
Follow the UPPER_SNAKE_CASE naming convention for constants
Know what happens when code tries to modify a constant

sections.text: In the Variables lesson, we saw that a variable's value can change: age = age + 1;. A constant is the opposite — a value that is fixed once set, and cannot be changed afterward.
A constant is a named value that is fixed at declaration and cannot be changed afterward during the program's execution.
This looks like a normal variable declaration, with one addition: the const keyword before the type. It means MAX_SCORE must be given a value right here, and that value can never change again.

sections.code: MAX_SCORE = 200; // compilation error — cannot assign to a const

sections.text: A const variable must be initialized at the moment it's declared. const int MAX_SCORE; with no value is not allowed — there would be no way to ever give it one afterward.

sections.title: Constants are useful when a value should never change
points: Fixed limits — a maximum score, a maximum number of attempts
Configuration-style values — a fixed tax rate, a fixed conversion factor
Protecting against bugs — the compiler stops you from accidentally reassigning it later in the code

sections.text: Marking a value const is a way of telling both the compiler and other programmers reading your code: this value is not meant to change. If someone tries to change it anyway, it's caught immediately as an error, rather than becoming a silent bug.

By convention, C++ constants are commonly written in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE — all capital letters, words separated by underscores. This makes them instantly recognizable as constants, distinct from ordinary variables like age or totalMarks.

sections.title: The speed limit sign analogy
sections.text: A speed limit sign says 60. Every driver reads that same fixed number — nobody gets to walk up and repaint the sign to say something else while driving. A constant in code works the same way: every part of the program reads the same fixed value, and nothing is allowed to change it.

Recall from the Compiler and #include topics: anything starting with # is a preprocessor directive, handled before compilation. #define creates a macro — wherever MAX_SCORE appears in the code, the preprocessor literally replaces it with 100 before the compiler ever sees it.

Notice #define has no = sign, no semicolon, and no type — it's pure text substitution, not a real variable. This makes it less safe than const: the compiler doesn't know MAX_SCORE is meant to be a number, it just blindly swaps text.

rows: Has no scope — applies everywhere after it, for the rest of the file

sections.text: For modern C++, prefer const over #define for constant values — it's type-safe and respects scope, which we'll study properly in a later topic.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Declaring const int MAX_SCORE; with no value — a const must be initialized at the point of declaration, since it can never be assigned afterward.
Trying to reassign a const after declaration — MAX_SCORE = 200; after const int MAX_SCORE = 100; is a compilation error.
Using lowercase for constants — not a compiler error, but breaks the UPPER_SNAKE_CASE convention that makes constants easy to spot.
Writing #define MAX_SCORE = 100; with an = and a semicolon — #define uses only a space between the name and the value, no = and no ;.
Assuming #define respects scope like a normal variable — it doesn't; it applies to the rest of the file from that point on.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a constant in C++?
questions.answer: A constant is a named value that is fixed at declaration and cannot be changed afterward during the program's execution.

questions.question: How do you declare a constant?
questions.answer: Using the const keyword before the type, and initializing it immediately, e.g. const int MAX_SCORE = 100;.

questions.question: What happens if you try to modify a const variable?
questions.answer: The compiler reports an error — a const variable cannot be assigned a new value after its initial declaration.

questions.question: What is the difference between const and #define?
questions.answer: const creates a real, typed variable handled during compilation and following normal scoping rules, while #define is a preprocessor text substitution with no type and no scope.

questions.question: Why is const generally preferred over #define for constants in modern C++?
questions.answer: const is type-safe and respects scope, catching mistakes the compiler can check, whereas #define is a blind text replacement performed before compilation even begins.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A constant is a value that's fixed once declared and can never be changed afterward. In C++, the modern way to declare one is with const, such as const int MAX_SCORE = 100;, which must be initialized immediately since it can't be assigned later. By convention, constant names are written in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE. An older approach is the preprocessor directive #define, which does plain text substitution before compilation and has no type or scope — const is generally preferred today because it's type-safe and integrates properly with the language.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Constants
sections.text: const = a locked locker — you set what's inside once, then the lock stays shut forever. Variable = unlocked locker, swap contents anytime.
